title: Quantifying information flow along a stochastic trajectory
authors: Yongjae Oh, Euijoon Kwon, Yongjoo Baek
abstract: Stochastic information flow (SIF) quantifies information flow at the trajectory level, overcoming the limitations of conventional symmetric, ensemble-averaged measures. However, computational difficulties have hindered the empirical application of the SIF. In this work, we propose a scalable deep-learning method for estimating the SIF from general time-series data. Its applications to an exactly solvable two-particle model, Kuramoto oscillators, and empirical trajectories of interacting motile cells demonstrate the utility of SIF as a data-driven indicator of cooperative structures.
summary: This paper introduces a scalable deep-learning method called NESIF to quantify directed information flow along individual stochastic trajectories. It overcomes the computational bottlenecks of traditional measures, allowing for the detection of hidden cooperative structures in complex systems like oscillators and biological cells. The method also establishes a formal link between information flow and stochastic thermodynamics.
main_problem: Quantifying directed information exchange at the trajectory level (Stochastic Information Flow) and overcoming the computational 'curse of dimensionality' when estimating these flows from high-dimensional time-series data.
main_result: The authors propose NESIF, a scalable deep-learning method that accurately estimates SIF, and demonstrate its ability to identify cooperative structures in Kuramoto oscillators and differentiate between cancerous and non-cancerous cell dynamics.
method: A neural estimation approach (NESIF) using artificial neural networks and alpha-divergence (alpha-MINE) to estimate Stochastic Mutual Information from time-series data.
model_or_system: The study utilizes several models: a two-particle Langevin system of overdamped Brownian particles, an N-bead harmonic chain, noisy Kuramoto oscillators, and empirical trajectories of interacting motile human epithelial cells.
key_observables: Stochastic Information Flow (SIF), Stochastic Mutual Information (SMI), scaled variance of SIF, entropy production rates, and stochastic area.
important_parameters: Temperature (T), coupling stiffness (K), observation period (tau), alpha-divergence parameter (alpha), and number of particles/beads (N).
main_assumptions: The current NESIF method assumes the system is in a steady state and that noise in bipartite Langevin dynamics is independent.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the anticorrelation between SIF and stochastic area with trajectory visualizations; Figure 2 validates the NESIF estimator using an N-bead model; Figure 3 and 6 analyze SIF statistics and synchronization in Kuramoto oscillators; Figure 4 compares information exchange in cancerous vs. non-cancerous cells.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the SIF concept, proposes the NESIF deep-learning estimator, validates it with analytical models (two-particle and N-bead), applies it to synchronization (Kuramoto), and finally demonstrates its utility on biological cell data.
why_interesting: The development of scalable, data-driven tools to quantify non-equilibrium information flow and entropy production is highly relevant for studying the dynamics of open quantum systems and many-body fluctuations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Histogram of time-integrated SIF JX versus stochastic area AXY of the two-particle model, collected for 106 trajectory segments with observation period τ = 1. The parameters used for the simulation are k = 2, K = 2, γ = 1, T = 2, and the discrete time step is fixed at ∆t = 0.001. The color bar indicates the number of trajectories inside each bin. (b) Covariance between the SIF and the stochas- tic area measured for 105 trajectories, as τ is varied. We also show the sample trajectories achieving the (c) minimum [JX(τ) = −4.472] and the (d) maximum [JX(τ) = 4.096] val- ues of JX(τ). The colors indicate the time evolution from the initial (blue) to the final state (red).

FIG. 2. (a) Schematic of NESIF. ∆t = 0.01. (b, c) Test results of NESIF using the N-bead model with k = K = γ = T = 1. (b) Comparison between the exact scaled variance DJx1 (solid line) and the values inferred using the NESIF (symbols), as the dataset size M is varied. (c) The coefficient of determination R2 between Jx1(τ = 1) and ˆ Jx1(τ = 1). In (b) and (c), the error bars denote standard deviation of 10 independent trainings, and the lines are to guide the eye.

FIG. 3. Mean (blue symbols) and variance (orange symbols) of the SIF from oscillators driven clockwise to those driven counterclockwise are plotted as functions of the driving fre- quency ω0, together with the synchronization order parameter r (green). The trajectory data are obtained by simulating 32 noisy Kuramoto oscillators with K = 1 using the time step size ∆t = 0.01 for the observation period τ = 10. Inset: Schematic of the Kuramoto model with oppositely driven os- cillators. The lines are to guide the eye.

FIG. 4. Sample trajectories and schematics of a pair of cells moving in an effectively one-dimensional channel, with (a) a reverse event and (b) a slide event highlighted by gray boxes. Data points are collected every one hour. (c) Frequencies of reverse and slide events and (d) mutual information between two interacting cells for each cell group. (e) SIF variance, with contributions from different types of collision events indicated by colors. (f) SIF variance per each collision event.

FIG. 5. (a,c) The scaled variance (DJx1 ) and (b, d) the coefficient of determination (R2) between ∆Jx1(τ = 1) and ∆ˆ Jx1(τ = 1), estimated by NESIF with different α. Dataset size is given by M = 105 in (a,b) and M = 106 in (c,d).
